title: Lifting and relaying a floorboard
subtitle: Remove a board for access or replacement without damaging the run
excerpt: How to cut free a tongue-and-groove floorboard, lift it without breaking adjacent boards, access the floor void, and relay the board cleanly. Covers detecting hidden services, cutting across the board at joist positions, and re-fixing without leaving a proud edge.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics (1880s).

content.text: without splitting adjacent boards requires cutting the tongue free before levering. Cuts across the board must land on a
so the cut end is properly supported when the board is relaid. The hidden-services check is non-negotiable: pipes and cables run in the floor void.

Before sawing, scan the floor with a cable-and-pipe detector in all directions across the board to be lifted. Mark any detections with chalk. Do not cut until the route is confirmed clear. In older houses, pipes and cables often run across the grain of the boards rather than along the void, making them invisible from the surface pattern.
Step 2, find the joists and mark cut positions

Tap across the floor: a dull thud indicates a joist beneath. Confirm with a thin nail driven through the board at the suspected position. Mark two cut lines across the board, each on the centre-line of the nearest joist to each end of the section to be removed. Both cut ends must bear fully on a joist so the relaid board does not spring in the middle.
Step 3, cut the tongue and cross-cuts

Run a utility knife along the groove side of the board to be lifted, pressing firmly, to cut the tongue free from the adjacent board. Repeat on the other side if the board is locked in from both sides. At each joist mark, cut across the board with a tenon saw set to a depth just exceeding the board thickness, typically 18 to 22 mm (3/4 to 7/8 in). Keep the saw blade against the joist edge so the cut falls on solid timber.

Drive a wide bolster or filling knife into the board edge at the cross-cut and lever upward. Work progressively along the board length rather than from one end only: levering from a single point risks snapping the board. On nail-fixed boards, punch the nail heads through the board from below once the board is slightly raised rather than pulling them up through the face.

Drill 3 mm pilot holes through the board at each joist position before re-fixing. Use 50 mm No. 8 Pozidriv screws rather than nails: screws allow future access without damage and eliminate the squeak that nails develop as the board moves seasonally. Set each screw just below the surface. If the board is slightly cupped, fix at both outer joist positions first to pull it flat before fixing at the centre.

attrs.fix: Stop levering. Run the utility knife the full length of the tongue line again, pressing firmly. Lift only once the tongue is completely free.
attrs.cause: The tongue was not fully cut before levering began.
attrs.symptom: Adjacent board cracks along its edge when levering

attrs.fix: Lift the board and check the joist top face is flat. Plane or shim any high points. Re-fix with screws rather than nails.
attrs.cause: The board is moving against a nail, an adjacent tongue edge, or a joist that is not flat.
attrs.symptom: Relaid board squeaks when walked on

attrs.fix: Fix a short nogging (timber offcut) between the joists directly under the cut end, or re-cut the board to land on the correct joist position.
attrs.cause: The cross-cut did not land on the joist centre, leaving the board end unsupported.
attrs.symptom: Cut end of relaid board sits proud or rocks
